coredata.dc:title: One-step green approach to synthesize highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from banana juice for selective detection of copper ions
coredata.dc:creator: Chaudhary, Navneet
Gupta, Pramod K.
Eremin, Sergei
Solanki, Pratima R.
coredata.dc:description: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from natural sources always remain an attraction due to their excellent optical properties. In present work, highly fluorescent N, S co-doped CQDs (NS-CQDs) were synthesized from banana (musa acuminata) juice using a simple one-step hydrothermal process, which not required any surface passivation, oxidizing agent or inorganic salt. Transmission electron microscopic images and X-ray diffraction analysis revealed spherical shaped NS-CQDs of an average size of 1.27 nm with poor crystallinity. The proposed method offers higher quantum yield (32 %) at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm as compared to other reported natural sources-derived CQD. These synthesized NS-CQDs utilized as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu (II) in the water sample. The fabricated fluorescent sensor displayed the detection of copper ions in the concentration range of 1−800 μg/mL with the LOD of 0.3 μg mL−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Green synthesis
Quantum dots
Fluorescent
Quantum yield
Heavy metal detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of aqueous dilutions of Cu2 +
Quantum yield and stability of prepared NS-CQDs
NS-CQDs functional groups and surface charge studies
Mechanism of the detection of Cu2+ with NS-CQDs
Effect of pH and selectivity of NS-CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are thankful to Advanced Instrument Research Facility (AIRF) JNU for facilitating the instrumentation facility. “This investigation was financially supported by project entitled “Rapid detection of bacterial resistance to antibiotics based on changing optical properties of nanosized labels” funded by Department of Biotechnology, India ( DBT/IC-2/Indo-Russia/2017-19/02 ) and Ministry of Education and Science of the Russian Federation in the frame of the State Targeted Program “Research and Development in Priority Areas of Development of the Russian Scientific and Technological Complex for 2014–2020” (contract 14.616.21.0061 from November 11, 2015; unique identifier of applied research: RFMEFI61615 × 0061).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of NS-CQDs synthesis synthesizing NS-CQDs from banana juice.
Possible mechanism of NS-CQDs of fluorescence quenching by Cu+2 ions.

(a) Linear plots between the abrorbance intensity and fluorescence intensity for NS-CQDs and Quinine sulfate to calculate fluorescence quantum yield; (b) Comparison of fluorescence quantum yield of banana juice derived NS-CQDs with other reported works [12,21–35]; (c) Fluorescence stability of NS-CQDs with respect to time (days).

(a) XRD spectra of NS-CQDs; (b) and (c) TEM images prepared NS-CQDs at lower and higher resolutions, respectively; inset of (b) shows a histogram for size distribution; (d) selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of NS-CQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of NS-CQDs, inset shows luminance of NS-CQDs under (i) daylight and (ii) high wavelength light; (b) fluorescence spectra of NS-CQDs for a range of excitation wavelengths from 280−350 nm; (c) cumulative peak fitting of UV–vis absorption of NS-CQDs.
(a) Complete XPS survey spectra of NS-CQDs; deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1 s, (c) S 2p, and (d) N1 s.

Change in fluorescence intensity with the Cu2+ concentrations from 1 to 800 μg/mL; (b) Calibration plot between Cu2+ concentrations and NS-CQDs fluorescence intensity; (c) Feasibility of NS-CQDs to detect Cu2+ from tap water.
(a) Selectivity of prepared NS-CQDs for various heavy metal ions, (b) change in NS-CQDs fluorescence under different pH conditions.
Comparison of performance of banana juice derived NS-CQDs to detect Cu2+ ions with some other reported CQDs based sensor for Cu2+ ions.

row.entry: Chemical method, use of hazards HCL, time 12 h.
Combustion, use of hazards nitric acid and sulfuric acid, 80 °C for 12 h.
N-CQDs from Citric acid and histidine
S, N-CQDs from citric acid and l-cysteine
1 to 800 μg/mL Or 5.5 μM to 4.4 mM

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Abbreviations: N-CQDs Nitrogen doped CQDs; S, N-CQDs sulphur and nitrogen doped CQDs; B, N-CQDs boron and nitrogen doped CQDs.

head.ce:title: One-step green approach to synthesize highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from banana juice for selective detection of copper ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Special Centre for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi, 110067, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Special Centre for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi-110067

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Enzymology, M.V.Lomonosov Moscow State University, Moscow, Russia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Enzymology, M.V.Lomonosov Moscow State University, Moscow, Russia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: A.N. Bach Institute of Biochemistry, Research Center of Biotechnology of the Russian Academy of Sciences, Moscow, Russia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Biotechnology of the Russian Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: A.N. Bach Institute of Biochemistry, Research Center of Biotechnology of the Russian Academy of Sciences, Moscow, Russia

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors have the same contribution.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from natural sources always remain an attraction due to their excellent optical properties. In present work, highly fluorescent N, S co-doped CQDs (NS-CQDs) were synthesized from banana (musa acuminata) juice using a simple one-step hydrothermal process, which not required any surface passivation, oxidizing agent or inorganic salt. Transmission electron microscopic images and X-ray diffraction analysis revealed spherical shaped NS-CQDs of an average size of 1.27 nm with poor crystallinity. The proposed method offers higher quantum yield (32 %) at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm as compared to other reported natural sources-derived CQD. These synthesized NS-CQDs utilized as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu (II) in the water sample. The fabricated fluorescent sensor displayed the detection of copper ions in the concentration range of 1−800 μg/mL with the LOD of 0.3 μg mL−1.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few years, incredible endeavors have been devoted to the detection and quantification of heavy metals in ecological and biological frameworks due to their conceivable lethal impact on the human being. Among the various heavy metals, Cu is recognized as the third most abundant heavy metal in the human body, which plays a crucial job in physiological procedures, dioxygen transport, activation, signal transduction, and energy generation [1]. The excess amount of Cu2+ in the human body can result in serious diseases such as Menkes and Wilson diseases, liver or kidney damage, hereditary aceruloplasminemia, gastrointestinal disturbance, and Alzheimer [2]. Cu2+ can enter the human body through water contaminated by industrial and consumer waste or by heavy metals released from soil breaking due to acidic rain. On considering potential impacts, the Environmental Protection Agency recognizes Cu2+ as an essential trace pollutant and sets a permissible amount of Cu in drinking water as 1.3 ppm or 20 μM [3]. Whereas, in human blood, its value exists in the range of 100–150 μg/dL (15.7–23.6 μM) [4,5].

Presently, there is incredible enthusiasm for growing new nanomaterials and minimal effort manufactured plans for developing reliable and sensitive analytical methods for the detection of Cu2+ in the ecosystem. There are various conventional analytical techniques available for Cu2+ detection, including potentiometric sensors, atomic fluorescence spectrometry, atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS), and coupled plasma mass spectrometry [6–10]. These analytical techniques are sensitive for Cu2+ detection but require relatively high-cost instruments, consume more time, toxic/hazards chemicals, and complex sample preparation. Now-days, fluorescent assay based sensors have been considered as the most suitable tool for the identification of heavy metals with the objectives of high sensitivity, selectivity, less interferences, cost-efficiency, and rapid response [11,12]. The fluorescent assay technique typically used fluorescent nanomaterials such as semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, which involved highly toxic precursors, multiple steps of synthesis, purification, and expensive chemicals for surface modification [13,14]. Therefore, it is crucial to develop a novel, facile, rapid, and cost-effective approach for developing biocompatible fluorescent nanomaterials.

In this scenario, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a smart and attractive family member of carbon nanomaterials, has replaced the semiconductor quantum dots due to its easy and rapid synthesis process, excellent water solubility, less toxicity, rich functional groups, high biocompatibility, and their outstanding physicochemical properties [15–19]. Synthesis of high-quality graphene QDs (GQDs) using simple, cost-effective, and environment-friendly method always remain an interest for the researchers. Commonly, synthesis of CQDs involves the carbonization of carbon sources and or quantum confinement of carbon materials such as graphene and carbon nanotubes. As compared to the confinement method, synthesis of CQDs from natural carbon sources (green synthesis) has great importance because it is straightforward, repeatable, environment-friendly, and financially savvy. The abundant availability of natural sources of carbon is also a reason for enormous attention for the green synthesis of CQDs [20]. So far, fluorescent CQDs were synthesized from a range of natural carbon sources including fruits, fruit juices, fruit peels, beverages, animal derivatives, bakery products, human derivatives, vegetables, spices, and plant leaves through hydrothermal, oxidation, microwave irradiation, heating and pyrolysis methods [12,21–35]. Moreira et al. [36] reported the synthesis of cuprizone functionalized fluorescent CQDs (C-CQDs) from sugarcane juice through the hydrothermal method, and applied for selective quantification of Cu2+. The prepared C-CQDs showed detection of Cu2+ in the range from 5.12 to 100 μmol/L with LOD of 0.76 μmol/L. In another study, Liu et al. [37] used hydrothermal method to synthesize CQDs from pear juice as carbon source and utilized for detecting a range of Cu2+ concentrations from 0.1 mg/L to 50.0 mg/L. Besides these, Tan et al. [38] synthesized CQDs from the carbonization of sago industrial waste using thermal pyrolysis method. They demonstrated linear detection of Cu2+ with LOD of 7.78 μM using standard Stern–Volmer quenching model. Among the aforementioned green synthesis methods, the hydrothermal method has been shown as a straightforward, cost-effective, environment-friendly, one-step, and efficient way for the synthesis of CQDs in an aqueous medium. The hydrothermal route also offers high yield and produces exceedingly proficient fluorescent probes for perceiving different compound species such as heavy metals in water. Thus, keeping given above banana juice is proposed as a natural source for synthesis CQDs using hydrothermal process. The banana juice contains a high amount of carbohydrates like glucose, fructose, sucrose and ascorbic acid as the carbon precursors [39,40].

The present work reports highly fluorescent N, S co-doped CQDs (NS-CQDs) derived from banana juice through the simple, cost-effective, one-step hydrothermal method. The structural and optical properties of synthesized CQDs were investigated using various techniques and finally applied for the detection of heavy metal (Cu) from water. The spherical shape NS-CQDs with the average size of 1.27 nm has revealed high solubility in aqueous media, blue emission, specific detection of Cu ions in the detection range of 1 to 800 μg/mL.

Banana (Musa acuminata) was purchased from the local market of Delhi, India. Absolute ethanol and quinine sulfate were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. Whereas, copper acetate, borate buffer, boric acid, sodium hydroxide, and other chemicals of reagent grade obtained from SRL Pvt. Ltd. India. All the chemicals were used without any further purification.

Fluorescent NS-CQDs from banana juice was derived through a simple one-step hydrothermal method. In a typical experiment, 80 gm of banana was cut into small pieces and turned into a paste with 100 mL of deionized water. Then 20 mL of the banana juice (pulp-free) was mixed with 20 mL of ethanol using continuous stirring. The above solution was transferred in Teflon linked stainless steel hydrothermal pot and placed in the muffle furnace at 150 °C for 4 h. The obtained dark brown product was further dissolved in the 20 mL of water and sonicate for 1 h, and the residue was separated by filtration. After that, 50 mL of ethanol was added into the aqueous filtrate and stirred for 30 min, followed by centrifugation (7000 rpm) for 15 min 2 times under ambient conditions to separate the large particles [41]. The water-soluble carbon dots with ethanol were kept in a vacuum desiccator for evaporation of ethanol at room temperature. Finally, obtained highly fluorescent NS-CQDs were stored at 4 °C when not in use. The nitrogen compounds and minerals present in banana juice are the sources of N, S doping in as-prepared CQDs [39,40].
The schematic representation of NS-CQDs production from banana juice is shown in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of aqueous dilutions of Cu2 +
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of Cu2+ (1–800 μg/mL) were prepared by dissolving copper acetate in deionized water and tap water, separately. During the detection, 50 μl of each concentration of Cu2+ ions was mixed with 2.7 ml borate buffer +200 μl of NS-CQDs stock solution (1.5 mg/mL), separately. The above solution was placed in a transparent quartz cuvette, and fluorescence intensity of solution was recorded in the range of 340–600 nm at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm.

Structure and morphology of prepared NS-CQDs were investigated using X-ray Diffractometer (Miniflex, Rigaku) and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2200 FS, Japan). The functional studies of NS-CQDs were examined using Fourier transform infrared microscopy (FTIR, Varian 7000). The optical studies (absorbance and fluorescence) of NS-CQDs in aqueous solution were recorded using T90 + UV/vis spectrometer and Cary Eclips fluorescence spectrometer, respectively. The quantum yield (Q.Y.) of the NS-CQDs was determined at an optimized excitation wavelength of 330 nm using the following formula:
Q C Q D = Q R .
I C D
I R
.
A R
A C D
.
ƞ 2 C D
ƞ 2 R
where ‘Q’ is the quantum yield, ‘I’ is the intensity of luminescent spectra, ‘R’ for reference is used in this equation, ‘A’ is the absorbance at exited wavelength and ‘η’ is the refractive index of the solvent used; using quinine sulfate (quantum yield 54 %), it is quinine sulfate Q.Y. used as reference in this line in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution as the reference. The compositional and elemental analysis of the samples was performed by X-Ray Photoemission Spectroscopy (XPS) using Oxford Instrument, with AlKα radiation (1486.7 eV) as an excitation source.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum yield and stability of prepared NS-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The NS-CQDs production yield was calculated to be 6.8 %, as 1.097 gm of the product was obtained from 16 gm of banana. Therefore, the above synthesis method can be proposed for large-scale synthesis of CQD. Moreover, the quantum yield of prepared NS-CQDs in aqueous medium was estimated using quinine sulfate as a reference at an excitation wavelength of 330 nm and found as 32 %. It can be easily seen that hydrothermal synthesized of NS-CQDs from banana juice has the highest quantum yield of 32 % as compared to other reported natural sources [12,21–35], as mentioned in Fig. 1 (b). The high pressure and temperature conditions under the hydrothermal reaction process are attributed to the efficient carbonization of carbohydrates of banana juice, and doping of N, S in CQDs matrix, as result a high QY. The fluorescence stability of NS-CQDs along the time (days) was observed using fluorescence spectroscopic measurements, and about 22.8 % decrease in the fluorescence intensity was observed in the 25 days.

The crystallinity of synthesized NS-CQDs was investigated using XRD. The diffraction pattern of NS-CQDs displays a broad peak centered at 17.1° corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic material. Whereas, the higher value of interlayer spacing of prepared NS-CQDs (d =0.52 nm) as compared to graphitic interlayer spacing (d =0.33 nm) indicates its poor crystallinity due to the adequate availability of oxygen-containing groups [42–44]. The TEM images visualized uniformly dispersed spherical shaped NS-CQDs with a diameter ranging from 0.5 nm to 2.5 nm. The average size of NS-CQDs was observed as 1.27 nm (inset of Fig. 2 (b)]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern also suggests the poor crystallinity of prepared NS-CQDs, which supports XRD results.

The optical absorption spectra of NS-CQDs show intense peaks in UV region centered at 284 nm and a tail outspreading in the visible region, which are correspond to the n–π* transition of the CO band and π–π* transition of the conjugated CC band, respectively [Fig. 3 (a)]. Under the high wavelength light, the prepared NS-CQDs display blue luminescence, as shown in the inset of Fig. 3(a). It was observed that the photoluminescence (PL) of NS-CQDs dependent on the value of excitation wavelength. Accordingly, by varying the excitation wavelength (280–350 nm), an intense luminescence of NS-CQDs was observed at 420 nm with the excitation wavelength of 330 nm [Fig. 3 (b)]. It is attributed that at a wavelength of 330 nm, the maximum number of NS-CQDs are excited. Besides this, the surface properties of NS-CQDs can also be responsible for this excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. To validate the existence of pi pi* and n pi* transition in NS-CQDs the UV–vis spectra fitted using origin program [Fig. 3 (c)]. The cumulative fitting of UV–vis absorption spectra using the gauss function reveals two separate peaks at around 284 nm and 330 nm. The intense absorption at 284 nm is correspond to π–π* transition of the conjugated CC band and less intense peak at 330 nm correspond to the n- π* transition of the CO band [45–48]. The functional groups associated with the NS-CQDs may generate a series of emissive traps between π and π* states of CC on its surface that lead the emission of NS-CQDs under the illumination at a specific excitation wavelength [49,50]. Hence, the fluorescence mechanism of NS-CQDs can be constrained by both the size impacts and surface imperfections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NS-CQDs functional groups and surface charge studies
ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups associated with the NS-CQDs were investigated using FTIR spectroscopic measurement [Fig. 4 ]. The FTIR spectra of NS-CQDs shows broad peak centered at 3324 cm−1, and a sharp peak at 2892 cm−1, which are attributed to the presence of OH/NH and CH group stretching [51]. The prominent peak appeared at 2975 cm‒1 corresponded to the methyl or methylene (CH) groups due to the existence of aliphatic hydrocarbons in banana [52,53]. Moreover, peaks at 1649 cm−1, 1385 cm−1, and 876 cm−1 were arisen due to the presence of CO, COO‒, and OO, respectively functional groups on NS-CQDs. The strong peaks in the range of 1240−1260 cm−1 and 1130–1000 cm‒1 were corresponded to CN stretching [54] and CO/SO stretching [55], respectively. The adequate availability of hydroxyl (−OH) and epoxide/either (COC) on the surface of NS-CQDs make it highly hydrophilic in nature in an aqueous medium.

Furthermore, the components of the prepared NS-CQDs are investigated using XPS characterization. The survey spectra of as-synthesized NS-CQDs reveals major peaks around 167.4, 284.9,400.7, and 532.0 eV correspond to sulfur (S 2p), carbon (C 1s), nitrogen (N 1s), and oxygen (O 1s), respectively [Fig. 5 (a)]. The deconvolution of C1 s spectra [Fig. 5 (b)] shows three peaks at 284.9, 286.5, and 289.0 eV link to CC, CN/CO/CS, and CO, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 (c), the high-resolution XPS spectra of S 2p exhibits five peaks centered at 163.7, 165.1, 166.5, 168.1, and 169.0 eV. The first two peaks are attributed to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of the -CS- covalent bond because of their spin-orbit couplings. [56] Whereas, last three peaks attributed to the -CSOx (x = 2, 3, 4) species. [57] Moreover, N1 s XPS spectrum exhibited mainly to pyrrolic N [Fig. 5 (d)]. The elemental ratio of O, C, N, and S was calculated as 21.09 %, 72.5 %, 2.29 %, and 4.1 %, respectively.

The uniformly dispersed aqueous solution of as-synthesized NS-CQDs was exposed to zeta potential measurement [Fig. 6 ]. The NS-CQDs rich with oxygenated functional groups (OH, COOH−, and CO) have shown a negative value of zeta potential (-22.67 mV). Based on this result, it can be attributed that negatively charged NS-CQDs along with vibrant functional groups, provided an excellent platform to bind Cu+2 ions. As result, aggregation of CQDS around the Cu+2 ions takes place that responded to the decrease in fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs (quenching). [58]

The feasibility of prepared NS-CQDs to detect Cu2+ was explored in terms of a decrease in fluorescence intensity (quenching) of NS-CQDs with Cu2+ concentrations (1–800 μg/mL). The change in fluorescence intensity was further observed by adding Cu2+ concentrations in the NS-CQDs aqueous solution, separately [Fig. 7 (a)]. It can be easily seen that Cu2+ concentrations efficiently quench the fluorescence of NS-CQDs. Moreover, an apparent red shift in emission peak detected with the addition of Cu2+ ions, which suggest the accumulation of NS-CQDs by interacting with Cu2+ ions. The calibration plot between Cu2+ concentration and NS-CQDs fluorescence intensity showed a subsequent decrease in NS-CQDs fluorescence intensity for Cu2+ concentration ranging from 1 to 600 μg/mL; after that, it got saturated [Fig. 7 (b)]. The prepared NS-CQDs has shown linear detection of Cu2+ ions in the range of 1–800 μg/mL with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.3 μg/mL. Here, LOD was estimated using formula 3S /m (S is the standard deviation of the corrected blank signals of the NS-CQDs, and m is the slope of the calibration curve). The excellent detection range falls into the permissible range of Cu2+ in drinking water as well as for industrial water.

The performance of banana derived NS-CQDs for Cu2+ detection is compared with the other reported CQDs based sensing of Cu2+. The banana juice derived NS-CQDs reveal broad linear detection range for Cu2+ detection compared to other reported CQDs based sensors, as mentioned in Table 1 . The prepared NS-CQDs also possess high quantum yield (32 %) as compared to green synthesized [33,36–38,59] and chemically synthesized [60–64] without the use of any surface passivation, doping agent, or hazards chemicals [62] [63]. The lower value of LOD (0.3 μg/mL) was observed than pipe tobacco [33], sugarcane juice [36], sago waste [38], d-glucose [65], and citric acid [66] derived CQDs based Cu2+ sensors. Although tuberose [59], grape juice [45], ethyle-nediaminetetraacetic acid [60], adenosine [61], polydopamine-polyethyleneimine copolymer [62], aminophenyl-boronic acid [63], and citric acid [64] derived CQDs based sensors for Cu2+ detection reveal lower LOD compare to NS-CQDs based Cu2+ sensor, but they required high temperature, consume more time, and expensive instrumentation. Furthermore, under same optimal conditions banana derived NS-CQDs were also applied for the detection of Cu2+ concentrations (0.1–600 μg/mL) in tap water [Fig. 7 (c)]. The prepared NS-CQDs have shown their high efficacy to recover of Cu2+ from tap water with the average RSD value of 1.06 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the detection of Cu2+ with NS-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above characterizations, it is confirmed that NS-CQDs possess negative (22.67 mV) surface charge and rich functional groups, including OH, COOH−, CO, and also may be few CN, and C–S [55]. It is attributed that negatively charged NS-CQDs along with vibrant functional groups (OH, COOH−, and CO) provided an excellent platform to bind Cu+2 ions. As a result, aggregation of NS-CQDs around the Cu+2 ions (complex formation by the coordinate bond) takes place as well as charge transfer between NS-CQDs and Cu2+ shows non-radiative recombination that responded to the decrease in fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs (quenching). [67] The possible mechanism is shown in Scheme 2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and selectivity of NS-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of proposed NS-CQDs for Cu2+ ions was studied in the presence of other potential interfering heavy metal ions related to the environment [Fig. 8 (a)]. During the study, the fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs was separately observed for a fixed volume (50 μl, 1 mM) of various metal ions, including Cu2+, Cu1+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and Ni2+. The plot of fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs for different metal ions demonstrates lower intensity in the case of Cu2+ ions compared to other ions. This behavior of NS-CQDs reveals that Cu2+ ions considerably quench the fluorescence of NS-CQDs, and the impact of other examined metal ions can be dismissed.

The change in NS-CQDs fluorescence was observed under different pH conditions 2–13, as shown in Fig. 8 (b). The result shows the maximum fluorescence intensity of banana derived NS-CQDs at pH 6. However, a decreasing trend in florescence intensity of NS-CQDs was observed at pH below 6 and above pH 8.

In this investigation, an easy and large-scale green synthesis of water dispersed fluorescent NS-CQDs from an inexpensive and promptly accessible natural precursor has been proposed. The green-synthesized NS-CQDs with the average size of 1.27 nm have shown blue fluorescence, and the highest quantum yield (32 %) as compared to other reported green synthesized NS-CQDs. Moreover, NS-CQDs have shown high selectivity (fluorescence quenching) for Cu2+ with respect to other heavy metals. The fluorescence quenching degree of synthesized CQDs has demonstrated a linear relationship with the concentration of Cu2+ in the range of 1–800 μg mL−1. Furthermore, high recovery of Cu2+ from spiked samples indicates a high analysis reproducibility of green-synthesized NS-CQDs for Cu2+ detection. It is suggested that the green-synthesized NS-CQDs can likewise be utilized in different novel applications, including biological applications and optoelectronics, because of low cytotoxicity, excellent dispensability, and tunable optical properties.

Mr. Navneet Chaudhary has synthesized the NS-CQDs and characterization work was equally shared by Mr. Navneet Chaudhary and Dr. Pramod Gupta. Cu ions detection idea by conceive by Dr. Pratima R. Solanki and Prof. Sergei Eremin. The complete manuscript was prepared by Mr. Navneet Chaudhary and Dr. Pramod Gupta and revised by Dr. Pratima R. Solanki.
The corresponding author is responsible for ensuring that the descriptions are accurate and agreed by all authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to Advanced Instrument Research Facility (AIRF) JNU for facilitating the instrumentation facility. “This investigation was financially supported by project entitled “Rapid detection of bacterial resistance to antibiotics based on changing optical properties of nanosized labels” funded by Department of Biotechnology, India (DBT/IC-2/Indo-Russia/2017-19/02) and Ministry of Education and Science of the Russian Federation in the frame of the State Targeted Program “Research and Development in Priority Areas of Development of the Russian Scientific and Technological Complex for 2014–2020” (contract 14.616.21.0061 from November 11, 2015; unique identifier of applied research: RFMEFI61615 × 0061).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design and synthesis of a fluorescent probe based on naphthalene anhydride and its detection of copper ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. He, C. Liu, X. Liu, Q. Wang, A. Fan, S. Wang, X. Qian, Design and synthesis of a fluorescent probe based on naphthalene anhydride and its detection of copper ions, PLoS One. 12 (2017) e0186994.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A DNAzyme catalytic beacon sensor for paramagnetic Cu2+ ions in aqueous solution with high sensitivity and selectivity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, Y. Lu, A DNAzyme catalytic beacon sensor for paramagnetic Cu2+ ions in aqueous solution with high sensitivity and selectivity, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 129 (2007) 9838–9839.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver nanoclusters as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Y. Lan, C. C. Huang, H. T. Chang, Silver nanoclusters as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions, Chem. Commun. 46 (2010) 1257–1259.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A simplistic approach to green future with eco-friendly luminescent carbon dots and their application to fluorescent nano-sensor ‘turn-off’ probe for selective sensing of copper ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Das, S. Ganguly, M. Bose, S. Mondal, A.K. Das, S. Banerjee, N.C. Das, A simplistic approach to green future with eco-friendly luminescent carbon dots and their application to fluorescent nano-sensor ‘turn-off’ probe for selective sensing of copper ions, Mater. Sci. Eng. C. 75 (2017) 1456–1464.
M. Araya, M. Olivares, F. Pizarro, Copper in human health, Int. J. Environ. Heal. 1 (2007) 608–620.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The Synthesis of a New Thiophene-Derivative Schiff’s Base and Its Use in Preparation of Copper-Ion Selective Electrodes
Electroanal. An Int. J. Devoted to Fundam. Pract. Asp. Electroanal.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Poursaberi, L. Hajiagha-Babaei, M. Yousefi, S. Rouhani, M. Shamsipur, M. Kargar-Razi, A. Moghimi, H. Aghabozorg, M.R.
